cards.front: What does an index do, and its cost?
cards.back: Speeds lookups/sorts on a column; costs storage and slower writes.
INNER returns only matches; LEFT returns all left rows, NULLs where no match.
WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY.
ACID — a group of statements commits all-or-nothing.
Primary uniquely identifies a row; foreign references a primary key in another table.
Collapses rows sharing a value into groups for aggregates (COUNT, SUM).
IS NULL / IS NOT NULL; "= NULL" is always unknown, never true.
Removes duplicate rows, keeping one of each unique combination.
One query per row in a loop instead of one set query — fix with a JOIN or IN.
Organizing tables to avoid duplicated data, so each fact lives in one place.
Sorts results by a column, ASC (default) or DESC.
LIMIT caps rows returned; OFFSET skips some — the basis of pagination.
COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX — summarize many rows into one value.
Pattern matching: % is any string, _ is one char. "abc%" = starts with abc.
A query nested inside another, e.g. WHERE id IN (SELECT ...).
Both stack result sets; UNION removes duplicates, UNION ALL keeps them (faster).
Inline if/else in a query to compute a value per row.
Returns the first non-NULL of its arguments — great for default values.
Use parameterized queries / prepared statements — never string-concatenate user input.
Shows the query plan — how the DB will execute it, so you can spot slow scans.
COUNT(*) counts rows; COUNT(col) counts non-NULL values in that column.
Inclusive range test: x BETWEEN 1 AND 10 means 1 <= x <= 10.
Matches any value in a list: WHERE status IN ('open','pending').
